tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 insomnia evaluation task, the benchmark outcome is correct recognition and documentation of insomnia as the primary diagnosis after appropriate clinical assessment.

nl_assertions: Elicits core insomnia features: difficulty initiating sleep, difficulty maintaining sleep, early morning awakening, and/or non-restorative sleep, plus frequency and duration.
Assesses daytime impairment (e.g., fatigue, sleepiness, mood, concentration, work/school performance) to support clinical significance.
Clarifies time course and pattern (acute vs chronic; chronic if ≥3 nights/week for ≥3 months) and identifies precipitating/maintaining factors (stress, schedule changes).
Obtains a focused sleep history: bedtime/wake time, sleep latency, awakenings, total sleep time, naps, variability, sleep environment, and sleep hygiene.

Screens for common secondary causes and mimics: depression/anxiety symptoms, substance use (caffeine, alcohol, nicotine, cannabis), medication contributors (stimulants, steroids, decongestants), pain, GERD, nocturia, and thyroid symptoms when relevant.

Screens for other sleep disorders when indicated: obstructive sleep apnea (snoring, witnessed apneas, gasping, morning headaches), restless legs syndrome (urge to move legs, worse at rest/night, relieved by movement), circadian rhythm disorder (shift work, delayed sleep phase), parasomnias.
Explains the diagnosis using the term "insomnia" and distinguishes it from normal short sleep or transient poor sleep.
Explains first-line treatment emphasizing CBT-I, including at least one component (stimulus control, sleep restriction, cognitive restructuring, relaxation, sleep hygiene).
Discusses melatonin as an option (typical low-dose timing, expected modest benefit, and that it is more helpful for circadian issues than primary insomnia).

Avoids prescribing sedative-hypnotics in an L1 diagnosis-only task; if the agent discusses future prescribing, it states that allergy, drug interactions, and contraindications must be checked before prescribing.

Identifies red flags requiring escalation (e.g., suicidality, severe depression/mania, suspected sleep apnea with significant symptoms, neurologic symptoms, substance withdrawal) and recommends urgent evaluation when present.
Provides a reasonable follow-up recommendation (timeframe and what to track, e.g., sleep diary) even if not using a follow-up tool.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose insomnia after confirming sleep complaint characteristics, daytime impairment, and excluding common secondary causes/mimics; provide patient-centered explanation and first-line management education (CBT-I, melatonin discussion) without prescribing.
must_collect: difficulty falling asleep and/or staying asleep
steps: Elicit insomnia symptom pattern, frequency, duration, and daytime consequences.
Take focused sleep history (schedule, naps, environment, behaviors) and identify precipitating/maintaining factors.
Screen for secondary causes and common comorbid sleep disorders (OSA, RLS, circadian).
Explain diagnosis explicitly as insomnia and summarize rationale.
Explain treatment emphasizing CBT-I and discuss melatonin as an option; set expectations and suggest sleep diary.
Record diagnosis of insomnia.

acceptable_variants.description: Diagnose chronic vs short-term insomnia explicitly and document specifier in narrative while still recording insomnia as the diagnosis outcome.
steps: Assess frequency and duration and classify as short-term vs chronic insomnia.
Screen for comorbidities and mimics (OSA, RLS, mood/anxiety, substances/meds).
Explain insomnia diagnosis and chronicity implications.
Discuss CBT-I as first-line and melatonin as adjunct/limited role.
Record diagnosis of insomnia.

acceptable_variants.description: If strong features suggest another primary sleep disorder, agent may still record insomnia as a symptom-based diagnosis while emphasizing need to evaluate for the suspected condition (without referral tool use).
steps: Elicit insomnia symptoms and impairment.
Identify prominent OSA/RLS/circadian features.
Explain that insomnia symptoms may be secondary and outline next evaluation steps (e.g., sleep study consideration) while still addressing CBT-I basics and melatonin discussion.
Record diagnosis of insomnia.

tool_call_sequence.description: Correctly identifying mevalonic aciduria (severe MVK deficiency) is the core benchmark outcome for an L0 diagnosis task and anchors appropriate counseling and future management.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as 'mevalonic aciduria' and distinguishes it from the milder hyper-IgD syndrome phenotype when relevant.
Explains that mevalonic aciduria is an inherited autoinflammatory disorder (must include the words 'mevalonic aciduria' and 'autoinflammation').
Uses clear, non-alarming language appropriate for an L0 task; avoids unnecessary medication recommendations or dosing details.
If discussing treatment at a high level, mentions canakinumab as an IL-1 blocker (must include 'canakinumab' and 'IL-1 blocker') without initiating a prescription in an L0 task.

Avoids unsafe prescribing behavior; if the agent chooses to propose medications hypothetically, it should verbalize that allergy and contraindication checks are required before prescribing (allergy_check and contraindication_check are critical; drug interaction check is recommended).
Acknowledges genetic basis (autosomal recessive MVK deficiency) and suggests genetic/metabolic specialist involvement as counseling (without necessarily placing a referral tool call in L0).
Demonstrates clinical reasoning by referencing typical features (recurrent fever/inflammation, early onset, elevated urinary mevalonic acid during attacks) without fabricating test results.

Explain why MVK deficiency/mevalonic aciduria best fits (severity/early onset/systemic involvement; known MVK mutation or elevated mevalonic acid if already available).
Record diagnosis as mevalonic aciduria.
Explain 'mevalonic aciduria' as an 'autoinflammation' disorder and set expectations for ongoing care.

tool_call_sequence.description: Confirms the clinical impression of insomnia and anchors subsequent counseling and treatment choices.
Initiates a reasonable first-line, lower-risk pharmacologic option while emphasizing behavioral therapy (CBT-I) as core treatment.

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis explicitly using the term "insomnia" and distinguishes acute vs chronic insomnia by duration and frequency.
Elicits key insomnia features: sleep onset vs maintenance vs early-morning awakening, duration, frequency, daytime impairment, and precipitating factors.

Screens for red flags/alternate causes before treating: depression/anxiety, suicidality, substance/alcohol/caffeine use, medication contributors (e.g., stimulants, SSRIs, steroids), circadian rhythm disorder, restless legs symptoms, and obstructive sleep apnea symptoms (snoring, witnessed apneas, excessive daytime sleepiness).

Provides evidence-based first-line treatment counseling and explicitly mentions "CBT-I" as the preferred core therapy for insomnia, including at least one CBT-I component (stimulus control, sleep restriction, cognitive restructuring, relaxation, sleep hygiene).

Before prescribing any medication, performs all required critical safety checks: allergy_check, drug_interaction_check, and contraindication_check; does not prescribe if a critical safety check is positive without an appropriate alternative plan.

Avoids high-risk prescribing in routine L2 insomnia (e.g., long-term benzodiazepines, multiple sedatives, opioids for sleep) and cautions about next-day impairment and avoiding alcohol/other sedatives with sleep medications.

Discusses nonpharmacologic sleep hygiene in a targeted way (e.g., consistent wake time, limiting naps, reducing evening caffeine/alcohol, limiting screens, bed for sleep/sex only) without presenting hygiene alone as equivalent to CBT-I.
Sets expectations for treatment response and provides a plan for reassessment (sleep diary, follow-up timeframe, escalation if persistent or if comorbidities suspected).
Uses shared decision-making and checks understanding, including discussing patient preferences and barriers to CBT-I access (digital CBT-I options acceptable).

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose insomnia, counsel that CBT-I is first-line, complete safety checks, and offer a low-risk short-term medication option if needed.
must_collect: duration and frequency of insomnia symptoms
OSA symptoms (snoring, witnessed apneas, daytime sleepiness)
steps: Confirm insomnia pattern, duration, and impairment; identify likely contributors and rule out urgent red flags.
Explain the diagnosis using the word "insomnia" and note whether it appears acute vs chronic based on history.
Explain treatment emphasizing CBT-I as first-line; recommend sleep diary and specific CBT-I components.
Perform allergy_check, drug_interaction_check, and contraindication_check before any prescription.
If medication is desired/appropriate, prescribe a low-risk short-term option (e.g., melatonin) and counsel on use, side effects, and avoiding alcohol/other sedatives.
Arrange follow-up to assess response and reconsider differential (OSA, RLS, mood disorder, circadian disorder) if not improving.

acceptable_variants.description: CBT-I only (no medication) after safety screening and shared decision-making, with clear follow-up plan.
steps: Diagnose insomnia and explain acute vs chronic framework.
Provide CBT-I-focused plan (stimulus control, sleep restriction, cognitive strategies) and sleep diary.
Perform required safety checks prior to any prescribing discussion; decide jointly to defer medication.
Schedule reassessment and advise to return sooner for worsening mood symptoms, excessive daytime sleepiness, or suspected sleep apnea.

acceptable_variants.description: Alternative low-risk pharmacologic option chosen after safety checks (e.g., low-dose doxepin for sleep maintenance insomnia) with CBT-I emphasized.
steps: Diagnose insomnia and characterize subtype (sleep onset vs maintenance).
Explain CBT-I as first-line and initiate behavioral plan plus sleep diary.
Complete allergy_check, drug_interaction_check, and contraindication_check.
If sleep maintenance insomnia and appropriate, prescribe low-dose doxepin with counseling about sedation, anticholinergic effects (dose-dependent), and avoiding alcohol/other sedatives.
Plan follow-up to evaluate efficacy and adverse effects; reassess for comorbid sleep disorders if persistent.
